All India Judges Association & Ors. v. Union of India & Ors. (2025 INSC 735)
📜 Theme: Upheld the three-year bar practice rule for Civil Judge (Junior Division) recruitment: ensuring judicial competence, professionalism, and independence under Articles 233–234.

👩‍⚖️ Bench:
Chief Justice Sanjiv Khanna,
 Justice B.R. Gavai,
 Justice S.V.N. Bhatti
📅 Judgment Date: May 7, 2025
⚖️ Jurisdiction: Writ Petition (Civil) under Article 32 of the Constitution.

📘 Background of the Case
Petitioners (law graduates and associations) challenged the eligibility rule introduced by several States — requiring a minimum of three years’ practice as an advocate to apply for the post of Civil Judge (Junior Division).
They argued that this rule discriminated against fresh law graduates, violated their right to equality (Art. 14) and freedom to practice any profession (Art. 19(1)(g)), and was contrary to the goal of expanding judicial access.
The Union of India, State Governments, and the All India Judges Association defended the rule, asserting that judicial office demands maturity, courtroom experience, and ethical grounding that can only come from legal practice.

📜 Constitutional Provisions (Explained Simply)
Art. 233 ⚖️ – Deals with appointment of District Judges; requires at least seven years’ practice as an advocate.


Art. 234 📘 – Recruitment of other judicial officers (below District Judge) through the Governor, in consultation with the High Court and Public Service Commission.


Art. 50 🏛️ – Directs separation of judiciary from executive control to secure independence.


Art. 14 & 19(1)(g) – Ensure equality and freedom to practice any profession, subject to reasonable restrictions.


Art. 309 🧾 – Allows rule-making for public services.



❓ Issues Before the Court
1️⃣ Whether the three-year bar practice requirement for entry-level judicial service violates Articles 14 and 19(1)(g).
 2️⃣ Whether such a condition amounts to arbitrary exclusion of law graduates.
 3️⃣ Whether the experience requirement enhances judicial quality and independence.
 4️⃣ Whether the All India Judges Association (AIJA) recommendations in 1993 and 2002 continue to hold binding value.

⚖️ Court’s Analysis & Reasoning
1️⃣ Judicial Experience Is Essential for Quality Adjudication ⚖️
 The Court emphasized that judicial officers are not mere administrators but guardians of constitutional rights. Practical exposure in courts builds understanding of procedure, evidence, ethics, and human conduct — indispensable for judicial functioning.
💬 “A judge’s training cannot begin at the bench; it must begin at the Bar.”
2️⃣ Valid and Reasonable Classification ✅
 Differentiating between law graduates and advocates with three years’ practice is a reasonable classification with a rational nexus to the object of ensuring competence. It does not violate Article 14.
3️⃣ No Violation of Article 19(1)(g) 🚫
 The rule does not restrict anyone’s right to practice law; it merely prescribes eligibility criteria for a judicial office. Such criteria are a reasonable restriction under Art. 19(6).
4️⃣ Implementation of AIJA (1993 & 2002) Directions 🧩
 The Court noted that the three-year experience rule was first proposed by the Shetty Commission and later approved in AIJA (2002). The 2025 judgment reaffirms those directions, ensuring consistency and quality across States.
5️⃣ Administrative Flexibility Retained 🏛️
 While upholding the rule, the Court clarified that States may consult their respective High Courts to adjust the eligibility condition if justified by local circumstances or candidate availability.
6️⃣ Judicial Independence Includes Professional Competence 🧠
 Judicial independence is not just about external autonomy but also internal integrity and competence. Professional experience ensures judges are better equipped to withstand executive or social pressures.
7️⃣ International Standards 🌍
 The Court compared India’s standard with the UK, Canada, and Australia — where prior legal experience is a universal prerequisite for judicial office.

💡 Doctrines & Principles Applied
Doctrine of Reasonable Classification (Art. 14) – Different treatment justified if based on intelligible criteria serving a legitimate purpose.


Doctrine of Proportionality – Restriction on opportunity is proportionate to the goal of judicial competence.


Doctrine of Judicial Independence (Basic Structure) – Institutional and personal integrity of the judiciary is part of the Constitution’s core.


Purposive Interpretation – Judicial appointment clauses (Arts. 233–234) interpreted in light of their purpose: to secure capable and independent judges.




⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
The three-year bar practice rule for recruitment to the Civil Judge (Junior Division) post is constitutionally valid, being a reasonable condition ensuring competence, professionalism, and independence in the subordinate judiciary.

🧾 Judgment / Directions
1️⃣ The three-year bar practice requirement upheld as valid and binding.
 2️⃣ No relaxation for fresh graduates unless State and High Court jointly decide otherwise.
 3️⃣ Union of India to coordinate with States and High Courts for uniform judicial recruitment standards.
 4️⃣ Judicial academies directed to strengthen post-appointment training modules.
 5️⃣ Recruitment Commissions to ensure merit-based, transparent, and periodic judicial selections.

📚 Key Precedents Cited
1️⃣ All India Judges Association v. Union of India (1993) – Directed separation of judiciary from executive and better service conditions.
 2️⃣ All India Judges Association v. Union of India (2002) – Introduced Shetty Commission reforms, including minimum practice requirement.
 3️⃣ Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India (2015) – Judicial independence is a facet of the basic structure.
 4️⃣ Union of India v. Madras Bar Association (2021) – Emphasized competence and tenure protection for judicial officers.
 5️⃣ State of Bihar v. Bal Mukund Sah (2000) – Judicial appointments must align with constitutional independence.

🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
The Supreme Court reaffirmed that the three-year practice rule for Civil Judge (JD) recruitment is a constitutional safeguard, not a barrier — designed to uphold the integrity, quality, and independence of the judiciary. ⚖️🎓

Amit Kumar v. Union of India (2025 INSC 414)
📜 Theme: Right to Mental Health & State’s Positive Obligation under Article 21

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice Sanjiv Khanna, Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia, and Justice Manoj Misra
Date of Judgment: 27 March 2025
Jurisdiction: Writ Petition (Civil) No. 157 of 2024

📘 Background of the Case
This case arose out of a series of student suicides in premier educational institutions — including IITs, NITs, AIIMS, and National Law Universities — which highlighted a national mental health crisis among young learners.
A public-spirited petitioner, Amit Kumar, filed a writ petition under Article 32, seeking directions to the Union Government to:
Formulate a uniform national policy for mental health and student well-being, and


Establish a National Task Force (NTF) to prevent student suicides, ensure counseling mechanisms, and monitor institutional accountability.


The petition also drew attention to discriminatory campus cultures, academic pressure, and the absence of qualified mental health professionals in most institutions — violating the right to life, dignity, and health under Article 21.

📜 Relevant Constitutional & Statutory Provisions (Explained Simply)
Article 21 – Right to Life and Personal Liberty → includes mental health, dignity, and emotional well-being.


Article 41 (Directive Principle) – Obligation of the State to secure the right to work, education, and public assistance in cases of sickness and disablement.


Article 47 – State’s duty to raise nutrition and standard of living, and improve public health.


Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 – Recognizes mental health as a statutory right and mandates mental health services as part of public healthcare.


UGC (Promotion of Equity in Higher Educational Institutions) Regulations, 2018 – Prohibit discrimination and ensure equal opportunity in universities.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether the right to mental health is an integral component of Article 21?


Whether educational institutions are constitutionally obligated to provide counseling and mental health support systems?


Whether the Court can direct the Union to frame a national policy and establish a centralized task force on student well-being?



⚖️ Arguments
🧑‍⚖️ Petitioner’s Contentions
Mental health is a constitutional right – Suicide prevention and psychological well-being flow directly from Article 21 and the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017.


Institutional neglect violates dignity – Failure to address systemic discrimination, academic pressure, and harassment infringes Article 14 and 21.


Need for central monitoring – Existing UGC and AICTE guidelines are fragmented; a national mechanism is required for uniform compliance.


🏛️ Respondent’s (Union Government) Contentions
The Government had already issued advisories to institutions; however, implementation varied.


They agreed that a Task Force model could enhance uniformity but sought reasonable time to formulate the framework.


The Ministry of Education and Ministry of Health & Family Welfare supported the idea of linking mental health helplines to educational institutions.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ Right to Mental Health = Core of Article 21 🧠
The Court reaffirmed that “health” in Article 21 extends beyond physical well-being — it includes the mental, emotional, and psychological balance essential to live with dignity.
2️⃣ Educational Institutions as Constitutional Actors 🎓
The Court declared that universities and colleges are “constitutional spaces” obligated to respect and facilitate students’ right to life and dignity. Neglecting mental health equates to institutional failure under Articles 14 & 21.
3️⃣ State’s Positive Obligation under Article 47 🌱
Citing Paschim Banga Khet Mazdoor Samity v. State of West Bengal (1996) and State of Punjab v. Mohinder Singh Chawla (1997), the Court reiterated that the State has an affirmative duty to provide adequate mental healthcare infrastructure and resources.
4️⃣ Policy Fragmentation & Institutional Apathy 🚫
The Bench noted that while certain universities had counseling centers, most lacked trained psychologists. The absence of standardized reporting on student suicides indicated a systemic governance failure.
5️⃣ Judicial Intervention within Constitutional Limits ⚖️
The Court clarified that directing how to implement policy would exceed judicial limits — but ensuring that the policy exists falls squarely within its Article 32 jurisdiction.

🧩 Doctrines and Principles Applied
Doctrine of Positive Obligation – The State must actively protect fundamental rights, not merely avoid infringement.


Doctrine of Parens Patriae – The Court acts as guardian of citizens’ welfare when the State fails to do so.


Right to Health and Dignity Doctrine – Established under CESC Ltd. v. Subhash Chandra Bose (1992) and extended to mental well-being.



🧾 Judgment / Directions
The Supreme Court partly allowed the petition and issued landmark directions:
Constitution of a “National Task Force for Student Mental Health (NTFSMH)” within 2 months.


Chaired by the Secretary, Ministry of Education.


Members from Ministry of Health, UGC, AICTE, NMC, and NLU Consortium.


The NTF shall:


Draft a National Mental Health Policy for Students (NMHPS).


Create mandatory counseling cells in all higher education institutions.


Establish a 24×7 helpline integrated with KIRAN (1800-599-0019) and tele-counseling services.


Submit an annual report to the Supreme Court for 3 years.


Universities to designate “Mental Health Officers” with authority to track and respond to crises.


UGC and AICTE directed to include “Psychological Safety Index” in accreditation parameters.



📚 Key Precedents Cited
Paschim Banga Khet Mazdoor Samity v. State of West Bengal (1996) 4 SCC 37 – Right to health as a fundamental right.


State of Punjab v. Mohinder Singh Chawla (1997) 2 SCC 83 – Health and medical care as part of Article 21.


CESC Ltd. v. Subhash Chandra Bose (1992) 1 SCC 441 – Human dignity includes physical and mental well-being.


Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985) – Right to life includes right to livelihood and dignity.


Unni Krishnan v. State of A.P. (1993) – Expanded scope of Article 21 to include education and welfare.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
The right to mental health and emotional well-being is an inseparable component of Article 21, and the State has a positive constitutional duty to prevent student suicides through a uniform, institutionalized mental health framework.

🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
The Supreme Court recognized mental health as a fundamental right, directing the creation of a National Task Force and a student mental-health policy, embedding psychological safety within the constitutional guarantee of life and dignity under Article 21. 🧠📘

Bharat Bhushan v. Union of India (2025 INSC 834)
📜 Theme: Clarified the constitutional scope of executive clemency and remission powers under Articles 72 and 161, emphasizing procedural fairness, transparency, and victims’ rights.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice Sanjiv Khanna
 Justice B.R. Gavai
 Justice S.V.N. Bhatti
📅 Judgment Date: 28 June 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Writ Petition (Criminal) under Article 32 of the Constitution

📘 Background of the Case
The petitioner, Bharat Bhushan, was a life convict in a murder case. He challenged the rejection of his remission application by the State Government, alleging that the decision was arbitrary, opaque, and politically influenced. He argued that it violated his right to equality (Article 14) and right to personal liberty (Article 21).
The case gained national significance because multiple remission controversies had arisen, including those involving commutations under Article 161 and selective releases of convicts, especially in high-profile or politically sensitive cases.
The Court treated the matter as an opportunity to clarify the limits and scope of executive clemency powers under Articles 72 and 161 of the Constitution.

📜 Relevant Constitutional & Statutory Provisions (Explained Simply)
Article 72 (President’s Clemency Power): Grants the President power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, or remissions for offences, particularly where the sentence is by a court-martial or death sentence.


Article 161 (Governor’s Clemency Power): Similar power vested in the Governor for offences against laws within the State’s executive domain.


Article 14: Mandates fairness and non-arbitrariness in State actions, including remission decisions.


Article 21: Guarantees life and personal liberty; ensures that punishment and mercy decisions must meet standards of due process and human dignity.


Sections 432–433A CrPC: Deal with remission, commutation, and premature release of prisoners by the appropriate government.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether executive clemency under Articles 72 and 161 is subject to judicial review, and if so, to what extent.


Whether remission policies must conform to constitutional principles of equality, reasonableness, and fairness.


Whether victims or their families have a right to be heard before remission or pardon is granted.


Whether political considerations in exercising clemency powers violate the Rule of Law doctrine.



⚖️ Arguments
🔹 Petitioner (Bharat Bhushan)
The rejection of remission was arbitrary and without recorded reasons, violating Articles 14 and 21.


Similar convicts in comparable circumstances were released, showing unequal treatment.


Clemency is an extension of Article 21 and must be exercised fairly, transparently, and without bias.


The power under Article 161 is not absolute, and courts can intervene in cases of mala fide or extraneous considerations.


🔹 Respondent (Union/State)
Clemency is an executive discretion and part of the doctrine of separation of powers; courts should not substitute their judgment for that of the executive.


Judicial review should be limited to cases of mala fide, arbitrariness, or non-application of mind.


The State claimed that its decision complied with the Remission Policy of 2018, approved by the Governor.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ Clemency is a Constitutional Duty, Not Personal Discretion
 The Court emphasized that Articles 72 and 161 confer constitutional powers, not personal privileges. These powers must be exercised in aid of justice and are subject to constitutional discipline.
2️⃣ Judicial Review Permissible but Limited
 While the decision to grant or deny remission is executive, the process leading to the decision is open to judicial scrutiny on limited grounds such as arbitrariness, discrimination, or mala fide.
3️⃣ Fairness and Transparency Required
 The Court held that States must record reasons for accepting or rejecting remission requests. Decisions must not appear capricious, opaque, or politically motivated.
4️⃣ Victim Participation Recognized
 The Court held that victims or their families have a limited right to be heard before remission or pardon is granted in heinous crimes. This aligns with the emerging Victim Rights Doctrine under Article 21.
5️⃣ Rule of Law and Article 14
 The Court stated that executive mercy cannot override the rule of law. Unequal treatment among similarly placed convicts violates Article 14.
6️⃣ Separation of Powers Preserved
 The judiciary will not substitute its decision for that of the executive. The Court’s role is to ensure constitutional compliance in the decision-making process, not to review the merits of the clemency itself.

🧩 Doctrines and Principles Applied
Doctrine of Limited Judicial Review: Clemency can be reviewed for procedural fairness and constitutional compliance.


Doctrine of Rule of Law: The exercise of mercy powers must be transparent and non-arbitrary.


Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation: Convicts are entitled to expect consistent application of remission policies.


Doctrine of Victim Participation: Victims’ voices must be considered as part of the justice process under Article 21.



🧾 Judgment / Directions
The State’s rejection of Bharat Bhushan’s remission was set aside for lack of transparency and reasoning.


The matter was remanded to the Governor for reconsideration in accordance with Article 161 and the prevailing remission policy.


The Court directed all States to:


Publish their remission and clemency policies on official websites.


Record written reasons for every clemency decision.


Provide a written opportunity for victims or their families to present their views before granting remission.


The Union Government was instructed to prepare uniform guidelines on the procedure for clemency and remission decisions.



📚 Key Precedents Cited
Epuru Sudhakar v. Government of A.P. (2006) 8 SCC 161 – Clemency is subject to limited judicial review.


Maru Ram v. Union of India (1981) 1 SCC 107 – Clemency is a constitutional power but must conform to the rule of law.


Kehar Singh v. Union of India (1989) 1 SCC 204 – President’s mercy power is broad but not immune from mala fide review.


Swamy Shraddananda v. State of Karnataka (2008) 13 SCC 767 – Human dignity continues through incarceration.


Laxman Naskar v. State of West Bengal (2000) 7 SCC 626 – Laid out parameters for premature release.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
The powers of pardon and remission under Articles 72 and 161 are not absolute. They must be exercised fairly, transparently, and consistently with the principles of the rule of law and equality, while ensuring limited victim participation in the process.

🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
The Supreme Court reaffirmed that clemency is a constitutional responsibility, not a political indulgence.
 Remission and pardon must conform to fairness, transparency, and equality, upholding both the dignity of the convict and the rights of victims under Articles 14 and 21. ⚖️✨

Imran Pratapgadhi v. State of Gujarat (2025 INSC 410)
📜 Theme:
 Freedom of Speech and Expression under Article 19(1)(a) — Constitutional protection of artistic and political expression and the mandatory preliminary inquiry for mid-range offences under Section 173(3) BNSS, 2023.

👩‍⚖️ Bench: Justice Abhay S. Oka
 📅 Judgment Date: March 28, 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Criminal Appeal / Writ Petition under Article 32

📘 Background
Imran Pratapgadhi, a Rajya Sabha MP and poet, shared a short poem on social media that spoke symbolically of justice, sacrifice, and peace.
 An FIR was registered in Gujarat under Sections 196 & 197 Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), alleging promotion of communal enmity and hatred.
 He argued that the poem was artistic and political expression protected by Article 19(1)(a) and that police action violated Section 173(3) BNSS, which requires a preliminary inquiry before FIRs in mid-range offences (punishable with 3–7 years).

📜 Relevant Provisions (Explained)
Article 19(1)(a): Right to freedom of speech and expression.


Article 19(2): Permits only reasonable and narrowly tailored restrictions.


Article 194: Legislative privilege limited to proceedings inside the House.


BNSS 2023 – Section 173(3): Requires a preliminary inquiry before registering FIRs in offences punishable 3–7 years, to prevent arbitrary prosecutions.


BNS 2023 – Sections 196–197: Punish deliberate acts promoting enmity or hatred.


Article 21: Due process and protection from arbitrary State action.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether registering an FIR without a preliminary inquiry under Section 173(3) BNSS was lawful.


Whether a poetic expression amounts to an offence under Sections 196–197 BNS.


Whether Article 194 protects a legislator’s extra-legislative speech.


How freedom of speech under Article 19(1)(a) interacts with public-order restrictions under Article 19(2).



⚖️ Arguments
Petitioner
The poem was literary and symbolic — no intent to incite hatred.


BNSS Section 173(3) makes preliminary inquiry mandatory; its violation vitiates the FIR.


The FIR was politically motivated and violated Articles 19 & 21.


Legislative privilege (Art. 194) is limited but his expression as a public representative deserves heightened constitutional protection.


State of Gujarat
The poem could disturb public order.


Preliminary inquiry is discretionary, not mandatory.


Privilege under Article 194 does not extend to social media.


The High Court was right in allowing investigation to continue.



⚖️ Court’s Reasoning
1️⃣ Interpretation of the Poem
The poem was a literary critique of injustice, not a call to violence. Mere emotional expression cannot be treated as criminal speech. Speech must be judged by context and intent, not public reaction.
2️⃣ Mandatory Preliminary Inquiry under Section 173(3) BNSS
The Court applied and extended the ratio of Lalita Kumari v. Government of U.P. (2014), holding that a preliminary inquiry is not optional but a procedural safeguard for offences punishable between 3–7 years.
 Since speech offences are often subjective and politically charged, the police must first verify if a prima facie case exists before registering an FIR. Failure to do so amounts to violation of Articles 14 and 21.
3️⃣ Ratio of Lalita Kumari (2014) Integrated
Registration of FIR is mandatory if information discloses a cognizable offence; however, preliminary inquiry is permissible and mandatory in ambiguous cases to avoid frivolous or politically motivated prosecution.
 The Court noted that Section 173(3) BNSS codifies this ratio, making it binding law for speech and mid-range offences.
4️⃣ Free Speech under Articles 19(1)(a) & 19(2)
Artistic and political speech enjoys enhanced protection. Citing Shreya Singhal v. UOI (2015), the Court held that only speech creating a direct and proximate danger to public order can be restricted.
5️⃣ Legislative Privilege (Article 194)
Privilege covers speeches made within the legislature, not on social media. However, the petitioner remains entitled to full constitutional free-speech protection as a citizen.
6️⃣ Judicial Standard – The “Reasonable Listener” Test
The Court introduced a new assessment standard: speech must be evaluated by how a reasonable, rational citizen would perceive it — not by how a hypersensitive or politically motivated listener reacts.

📗 Guidelines for Future Cases
For offences punishable 3–7 years under BNSS, a preliminary inquiry is normally required.


For speech offences linked to Article 19(2), a preliminary inquiry is mandatory.


Courts must quash FIRs that are mechanical or politically motivated.


State Governments must train police officers on constitutional limits governing speech and due process.


FIRs filed without following Section 173(3) BNSS will be procedurally invalid.



📚 Key Precedents Cited
Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of U.P. (2014) 2 SCC 1 – FIR mandatory if cognizable offence is disclosed; preliminary inquiry permissible for ambiguous cases.


Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) – Speech restrictions must be clear and proximate.


Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar (1962) – Criticism of government not sedition unless incitement to violence.


Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) – Fair procedure is part of Article 21.


Neeharika Infrastructure v. State of Maharashtra (2021) – FIRs can be quashed to prevent rights violation.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
The registration of an FIR without conducting a preliminary inquiry for speech-related offences punishable up to seven years violates Section 173(3) BNSS and Articles 19 and 21.
 This judgment applies the Lalita Kumari ratio to the BNSS regime, transforming a judicial guideline into a constitutional safeguard for free expression and due process.

💡 Doctrinal and Constitutional Significance
Lalita Kumari Doctrine → Statutory Codification: Section 173(3) BNSS formalizes preliminary inquiry rules.


Procedural Due Process: Prevents weaponization of criminal law against speech.


Proportionality & Fairness: Restrictions must be proportionate and justified by evidence.


Freedom of Expression: Reiterated as a core democratic value under Article 19(1)(a).



🧾 Judgment / Directions
FIR against Imran Pratapgadhi quashed for lack of preliminary inquiry.


Mandatory compliance with Section 173(3) BNSS in future speech-related cases.


Training of police officers on free-speech jurisprudence and procedural rights.


Periodic monitoring by High Courts to check politically motivated FIRs.



🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
Imran Pratapgadhi v. State of Gujarat (2025) applied the Lalita Kumari ratio to the BNSS framework, holding that speech-related FIRs require a preliminary inquiry and that artistic or political expression cannot be criminalized without due process. 🗣️⚖️

In Re: Rights of Students with Disabilities (2025 INSC 991)
📜 Theme:
 Inclusive education as a constitutional obligation under Articles 14, 19(1)(a), 21 and 21-A; affirmative implementation of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act) within universities and competitive examinations.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice D.Y. Chandrachud (CJI)
 Justice J.B. Pardiwala
 Justice Manoj Misra
📅 Judgment Date: 7 August 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Suo Motu Public Interest Proceedings under Article 32

📘 Background of the Case
The Supreme Court took suo motu cognizance after a visually impaired law student was denied scribe and reasonable accommodation in a National Entrance Test.
 Reports revealed a pattern of discrimination against students with disabilities in central and state universities — missing accessible infrastructure, inadequate scribes, and non-functional Disability Cells.
Treating this as a violation of Articles 14 and 21, the Court undertook a holistic review of the constitutional and statutory obligations toward students with disabilities.

📜 Relevant Provisions (Explained Simply)
Article 14: Guarantees equal protection — requires positive discrimination for disadvantaged classes.


Article 21: Right to life includes dignity, education, and participation without discrimination.


Article 21-A: Free and compulsory education for children 6–14; interpreted broadly for inclusive education.


Articles 41 & 46 (DPSPs): Mandate State to make effective provisions for education and promotion of weaker sections.


Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act):


Section 16: Duties of educational institutions to provide reasonable accommodation and inclusive education.


Section 31: Right to access higher education without discrimination.


Section 34: Reservation in employment and education for PwDs.


UGC (Accessibility to Higher Education for PwDs) Regulations, 2017 – require each university to set up Equal Opportunity Cells and Disability Resource Centres.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether the denial of accommodation to students with disabilities violates Articles 14, 19 and 21.


Whether universities and public authorities have a positive constitutional obligation to create accessible environments.


Whether failure to implement the RPwD Act amounts to constitutional tort and breach of fundamental rights.



⚖️ Arguments
🔹 Amicus Curiae and Petitioners (Students and NGOs)
Non-implementation of Sections 16 and 31 RPwD Act has denied equal access to education.


Denial of scribes, assistive technology and relaxation in exams violates Article 21’s dignity and Article 14’s equality.


Inclusive education is a facet of right to life and liberty, not mere policy.


🔹 Respondents (Union of India & UGC)
UGC guidelines exist but enforcement rests with universities.


State funding constraints delay infrastructure adaptation.


Sought time to formulate a National Accessibility Framework.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ Constitutional Duty of Inclusion
Education without accessibility is illusory.
 The Court held that the State’s obligation under Articles 14, 21 and 21-A includes ensuring physical, digital, and academic accessibility.
 Equality demands not sameness but reasonable differentiation to achieve substantive fairness.
2️⃣ Dignity and Autonomy as Core of Article 21
The Court stressed that freedom to learn and participate in society is integral to dignity.
 Denial of reasonable accommodation is not just statutory non-compliance but a constitutional violation.
3️⃣ RPwD Act as Transformative Legislation
The Act operationalizes India’s commitment to the UN Convention on Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD).
 Statutory duties under Sections 16 and 31 are mandatory, not directory.
 Non-compliance amounts to dereliction of constitutional duty under Article 41.
4️⃣ Institutional Accountability
Universities cannot plead budgetary constraints to deny rights.
 The Court directed UGC and AICTE to monitor implementation through bi-annual audits.
5️⃣ Right to Assistive Technology and Digital Access
Access to technology (scribes, Braille, screen-reading software, captioning) was declared a facet of Article 21 and Article 19(1)(a) (freedom to receive information).
6️⃣ Comparative Perspective
The Court referred to U.S. ADA (1990) and the U.K. Equality Act (2010) to underscore State obligations in creating inclusive academic spaces.

📗 Directions Issued
National Framework: The Union Government shall notify a National Accessibility Framework for Higher Education within 3 months.


University Disability Cells: Every university to establish a functional Disability Cell with trained staff within 6 months.


Scribe and Exam Support: Standardized Scribe Guidelines to be made uniform across boards and entrance tests.


Technology Access: All exam and learning platforms to comply with Digital Accessibility Standards (DAISY, WCAG 2.1).


Monitoring: UGC and AICTE to file compliance reports every six months; non-compliance to invite contempt.



📚 Precedents Cited
Jeeja Ghosh v. Union of India (2016) 7 SCC 761 – Human dignity of disabled persons is non-negotiable.


Vikash Kumar v. UPSC (2021) 5 SCC 370 – Reasonable accommodation under RPwD Act is a statutory right, not charity.


State of Kerala v. Leesamma Joseph (2021) 6 SCC 757 – Denial of promotion to disabled employee violates Article 14.


Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018) 10 SCC 1 – Equality includes the right to be different.


Paschim Banga Khet Mazdoor Samity v. State of West Bengal (1996) 4 SCC 37 – Right to health and medical care forms part of Article 21.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
Denial of reasonable accommodation and failure to ensure inclusive infrastructure violate Articles 14 and 21.
 Sections 16 and 31 of the RPwD Act create enforceable rights, not policy goals.
 Educational institutions carry a constitutional and statutory duty to provide access and reasonable adjustments for students with disabilities.

💡 Doctrinal Insights
Doctrine of Reasonable Accommodation: Core to substantive equality.


Transformative Constitutionalism: Interpreting the Constitution as a living document to empower the marginalized.


Positive Obligation of State: The State must act affirmatively, not merely refrain from discrimination.


Participatory Equality: Equality achieved through inclusion and active support, not formal uniformity.



🧾 Judgment Summary (Short Form)
The Supreme Court declared inclusive education a fundamental right, holding that universities and exam bodies are under a positive duty to provide reasonable accommodation, assistive technology, and equal learning opportunities for students with disabilities.
 Failure to comply amounts to a violation of Articles 14, 19, 21, and 21-A. 🧑‍🎓♿⚖️

In Re: Right to Privacy of Adolescents (2025 INSC 778)
📜 Theme:
 Reaffirmation of adolescent privacy and welfare as part of Article 21; limits on judicial discretion in sexual-offence cases involving minors; and the scope of Article 142 for equitable rehabilitation.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice Abhay S. Oka
 Justice Ujjal Bhuyan
📅 Judgment Date: 23 May 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Suo Motu Criminal Proceedings and State Appeals (arising from Calcutta High Court judgment dated 18 October 2023)

📘 Background of the Case
The case originated when the Calcutta High Court acquitted a man convicted under Section 6 of the POCSO Act, 2012 (aggravated penetrative sexual assault) and Sections 363 and 366 IPC (kidnapping and abduction), on the ground that the 14-year-old girl was in a “consensual relationship.”
 The High Court made controversial comments about adolescent sexual behavior.
The Supreme Court took suo motu cognizance, finding such remarks deeply inappropriate and contrary to the protective spirit of the POCSO Act. It also considered a State appeal against the acquittal.

📜 Relevant Provisions (Explained Simply)
Indian Penal Code, 1860
Section 363: Kidnapping from lawful guardianship.


Section 366: Abduction of a woman to compel marriage or illicit intercourse.


Section 376(3): Punishment for rape of a woman under sixteen.


Section 376(2)(n): Repeated rape on the same woman.


POCSO Act, 2012
Section 6: Punishment for aggravated penetrative sexual assault (minimum 20 years to life).


Section 19(6): Duty to report offences; confidentiality of victim identity and statements.


Constitution of India
Article 21: Right to life includes privacy, dignity, and rehabilitation of victims.


Article 142: Power of Supreme Court to pass any order to do “complete justice.”


Article 39(e): Directive to ensure protection of children against exploitation.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether consensual relationships can ever justify acquittal under the POCSO Act.


Whether the High Court erred by misusing its powers under Article 226 and Section 482 CrPC.


Whether Article 142 can be used to modify sentencing in the interest of victim rehabilitation.


Whether the State has a constitutional duty to rehabilitate minor victims and their children.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ Consent of a Minor Is No Defence
The Court reaffirmed that minors are legally incapable of giving consent under the POCSO Act.
 Any sexual act with a person under 18 years — even if voluntary — is statutory rape.
 The High Court’s reliance on “consensual relationship” was legally unsustainable and undermined the object of child protection.
2️⃣ Misuse of Judicial Discretion
The Supreme Court criticized the High Court for making sociological remarks on adolescent sexuality and for exceeding its jurisdiction under Article 226 and Section 482 CrPC.
 Courts must apply the law, not moral or personal theories of relationships.
3️⃣ Constitutional Duty of Rehabilitation
Justice Oka stressed that the State’s responsibility does not end with conviction.
 Under Article 21, the victim’s right to rehabilitation, education, and privacy continues throughout her life.
 The State is a guardian (parens patriae) for every child under the protective constitutional framework.
4️⃣ Article 142 and Holistic Justice
The Court invoked Article 142 to craft a nuanced relief —
 Although the convict’s guilt was affirmed under Section 376 IPC and Section 6 POCSO, his sentence was modified to time already served, considering:
the victim’s current life situation (living with the convict and their child),


the need for stability for the child’s welfare, and


the victim’s stated wish not to disrupt her family.


The Court clarified that this is an exceptional, non-precedential exercise of equitable jurisdiction.
5️⃣ Failure of Welfare State
The Court observed that the case revealed a failure of the constitutional welfare model — the victim lacked proper protection, rehabilitation, or financial aid.
 It directed the State to act as a “true guardian” and ensure educational and financial support.

📚 Precedents Cited and Their Application
Gian Singh v. State of Punjab (2012) 10 SCC 303 – Power to quash criminal proceedings in serious offences must be used cautiously; rape cases not compoundable.


Shilpa Sailesh v. Varun Sreenivasan (2023) 14 SCC 231 – Clarified the scope of Article 142 for doing complete justice; relied on here to justify limited sentencing discretion.


Elumalai v. Inspector of Police (2022) – Article 142 used to commute sentence considering victim’s welfare post-marriage and childbirth.


K. Dhandapani v. State (2022 SCC OnLine SC 1056) – Emphasized proportional justice in POCSO sentencing when rehabilitation outweighs punishment.


Independent Thought v. Union of India (2017) 10 SCC 800 – Read down marital rape exception; reaffirmed minor’s bodily autonomy.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
Consent of a minor is legally irrelevant under the POCSO Act; any sexual act with a person below 18 years constitutes statutory rape.


Courts cannot use powers under Articles 226 or 482 CrPC to quash statutory offences based on “consensual adolescent relationships.”


Article 142 empowers the Supreme Court to tailor justice to protect victim welfare without undermining statutory rigour — but such relief is case-specific and non-precedential.


The State has a continuing constitutional obligation to ensure rehabilitation, privacy, and education of child victims and their offspring.



🧾 Directions Issued
The convict’s conviction under Section 6 POCSO and Section 376 IPC was upheld.


Sentence reduced to period already served, using Article 142 powers.


The State of West Bengal directed to:


Provide the victim and her child safe housing, financial support, and education.


Establish a Child Victim Rehabilitation Fund for long-term assistance.


File compliance reports every six months.


Union Ministry of Women and Child Development to constitute a National Committee to review adolescent rights under POCSO and recommend reforms by July 2025.



💡 Doctrinal and Conceptual Insights
Article 21 – Expanding Horizon: Protects not only bodily integrity but emotional rehabilitation and privacy of minors.


Doctrine of Parens Patriae: The State acts as a guardian for unprotected children.


Doctrine of Transformative Justice: Legal outcomes must prioritize rehabilitation over retribution in child-protection contexts.


Balance between Statutory Rigour and Human Welfare: Article 142 allows equitable solutions without eroding the POCSO framework.


Privacy of Adolescents: Extends Puttaswamy (2017) privacy principles to minors in conflict and protection cases.



📗 Guidelines for Future Cases
No compromise or quashing permissible in POCSO or rape cases, even if victim and accused cohabit.


Article 142 discretion must be used only for rehabilitative purposes, not to dilute guilt.


State Governments must create structured rehabilitation frameworks for minor victims.


Media and authorities must ensure confidentiality of victim identity and future.



🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
In Re: Right to Privacy of Adolescents (2025) reaffirmed that minors’ privacy, dignity, and rehabilitation form an inalienable part of Article 21. The Court upheld statutory rigour under POCSO but, under Article 142, ensured equitable relief and State guardianship — marking a new phase in India’s adolescent-rights jurisprudence. 👩‍⚖️🧒⚖️

Poulomi Pavini Shukla v. Union of India (2025 INSC 552)
📜 Theme:
 Recognition of orphans as a “disadvantaged group” under Article 21A and Section 2(d) of the RTE Act, 2009 — advancing the doctrine of substantive equality and welfare obligations of the State.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice Surya Kant
 Justice Sandeep Mehta
📅 Judgment Date: 2 April 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Writ Petition (Civil) under Article 32 of the Constitution

📘 Background of the Case
The petition, filed by advocate and child rights activist Poulomi Pavini Shukla, highlighted the systemic neglect of orphaned and abandoned children in education policy.
Despite numerous government welfare schemes, orphans were not recognized as a “disadvantaged group” under Section 2(d) of the Right to Education (RTE) Act, 2009, depriving them of the 25% reserved seats in private schools mandated for weaker and disadvantaged sections.
The petitioner sought:
Recognition of orphans as a statutorily protected class;


Implementation of Article 21A and Article 14 in letter and spirit;


Creation of a national policy ensuring their access to education, shelter, and scholarships.



📜 Relevant Provisions (Explained Simply)
Article 14: Equality before law includes substantive equality — treating unequals differently to achieve fairness.


Article 15(3): Permits special provisions for vulnerable groups (like children).


Article 21: Protects life, dignity, and education as inseparable rights.


Article 21A: Mandates free and compulsory education for children aged 6–14.


Directive Principles (Articles 38, 39(e), 41): Direct the State to protect childhood and promote equal opportunity.


Section 2(d), RTE Act, 2009: Defines “child belonging to disadvantaged group” — originally including SC/ST, socially and educationally backward classes, and differently-abled children.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether orphans constitute a “disadvantaged group” under Section 2(d) of the RTE Act.


Whether failure to include orphans violates Articles 14 and 21A.


Whether the State is constitutionally obliged to provide special protection to children without parental support.



⚖️ Arguments
🔹 Petitioner (Poulomi Pavini Shukla):
Orphans, being parentless and voiceless, face systemic exclusion from education.


Their exclusion from RTE reservations is discriminatory and violates Article 14.


Article 21A read with Article 15(3) and Article 39(e) imposes an affirmative duty to protect childhood.


The State cannot rely on welfare schemes alone — statutory recognition ensures enforceable rights.


🔹 Respondents (Union of India & State Governments):
Welfare schemes under the Juvenile Justice Act (JJA) and ICPS (Integrated Child Protection Scheme) already exist.


Inclusion of orphans under the RTE Act requires legislative amendment — not judicial direction.


The issue falls within executive domain.



⚖️ Court’s Observations & Reasoning
1️⃣ Constitutional Meaning of “Disadvantaged Group”
The Court held that the phrase “child belonging to disadvantaged group” under Section 2(d) of the RTE Act must be interpreted purposively, in light of Articles 14 and 21A.
 The Constitution is not limited to formal equality — it envisions substantive equality, requiring protection for those who cannot protect themselves.
2️⃣ Orphans as a Vulnerable Class
Orphans are among the most vulnerable sections — lacking family, representation, and access to schemes.
 Their exclusion from the statutory category is arbitrary, unreasonable, and contrary to Article 14.
3️⃣ Positive Obligations under Article 21A
Education for orphans is not charity but a fundamental right.
 The Court relied on Mohini Jain (1992) and Unni Krishnan (1993) to reaffirm that the right to education flows directly from the right to life with dignity.
4️⃣ Harmonious Reading with JJ Act and RTE Act
The Court harmonized the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 and the RTE Act, holding that both aim at the rehabilitation and reintegration of children.
 Excluding orphans from RTE’s protective ambit undermines the JJ Act’s objectives.
5️⃣ Transformative Interpretation
Justice Surya Kant emphasized that social justice provisions must be read progressively — constitutional guarantees evolve to meet modern inequalities.

📚 Key Precedents Cited
Unni Krishnan v. State of Andhra Pradesh (1993) 1 SCC 645 – Education is a facet of Article 21.


Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka (1992) 3 SCC 666 – Right to education flows from right to life and dignity.


National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India (2014) 5 SCC 438 – Recognition of identity and vulnerability as the basis for substantive equality.


Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018) 10 SCC 1 – Expanding equality and dignity to marginalized groups.


Vikash Kumar v. UPSC (2021) 5 SCC 370 – Affirmative accommodation is not charity but enforceable entitlement.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
Children without parental or family support constitute a “disadvantaged group” under Section 2(d) of the RTE Act.
 The right to education under Article 21A includes a positive obligation on the State to identify and provide free, quality, and inclusive education to orphans.
 Failure to do so violates Articles 14, 15(3), and 21.

🧾 Judgment / Directions
Statutory Inclusion: The Court directed the Union Government to formally amend Section 2(d) of the RTE Act to include orphans as a “disadvantaged group.”


Reservation Mandate: Orphans are entitled to the 25% reservation in private unaided schools under Section 12(1)(c) RTE Act.


Educational Welfare Scheme: The Ministry of Education to create a Central Orphan Education Fund, ensuring scholarships, hostel facilities, and counselling support.


State Compliance: States must maintain district-level databases of orphaned children eligible for free education.


Periodic Monitoring: The Court ordered annual compliance affidavits to be filed before the Supreme Court.



💡 Doctrinal Significance
Substantive Equality: Equality demands proactive inclusion of the marginalized, not formal neutrality.


Parens Patriae Doctrine: The State acts as guardian for those without parental care.


Transformative Constitutionalism: Judicial interpretation must advance the Constitution’s moral mission of inclusion.


Positive Obligation of the State: Fundamental rights entail enforceable duties, not mere non-interference.



🧩 Conceptual Link to CLAT-PG Themes
Expands Article 21A → “Right to Education = Right to Equal Educational Opportunity.”


Integrates Article 14 with affirmative action principles under Article 15(3).


Strengthens welfare jurisprudence in line with Vikash Kumar (PwD) and Jeeja Ghosh cases.



🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
Poulomi Pavini Shukla v. Union of India (2025) transformed India’s educational equality jurisprudence by declaring orphans as a “disadvantaged group” under the RTE Act, making their inclusion in free and compulsory education a constitutional mandate under Articles 14, 21 and 21A. 🎓⚖️

Radhika Agarwal v. Union of India & Ors. (2025 INSC 272)
🎯 Arrest under GST & Customs Acts valid only if procedural safeguards are strictly followed

👩‍⚖️ Bench: 
Chief Justice Sanjiv Khanna (majority)
Justice M.M. Sundresh
Justice Bela M. Trivedi (concurring)
Jurisdiction: Criminal Original Jurisdiction – Writ Petitions & Criminal Appeals

📜 Statutes & Provisions Involved (with Meaning)
🧾 Customs Act 1962
sec. 104(1) – Allows officers to arrest if there are reasons to believe an offence was committed.


sec. 104(4)–(7) – Defines which offences are cognisable / non-cognisable and bailable / non-bailable (only serious evasion > ₹50 lakh permits arrest without warrant).


💼 Central Goods and Services Tax Act 2017
sec. 67 – Authorises search & seizure on reasonable belief of tax evasion.


sec. 69 – Empowers Commissioner to authorise arrest for major GST offences.


sec. 70 – Allows summons for evidence or document production.


sec. 132 – Lists GST offences and punishments; evasion > ₹5 crore = cognisable and non-bailable.


⚖️ Criminal Procedure Code 1973
sec. 41B 📄 – Arrest memo with witness signature & officer ID.


sec. 41D 🧑‍⚖️ – Right to meet advocate during interrogation (within sight).


sec. 50A 📞 – Inform family or nominee about arrest.


sec. 55A 🏥 – Ensure arrestee’s health & safety.


sec. 167 ⏱️ – Produce arrestee before Magistrate within 24 hours.


📘 Constitution of India
Art. 14 ⚖️ – Guards against arbitrariness.


Art. 20(3) 🚫 – Right against self-incrimination.


Art. 21 🕊️ – Personal liberty through fair procedure.


Art. 22(1) 📜 – Right to be told grounds of arrest & consult a lawyer.


Art. 246A 🏛️ – Parliament & States share power to make GST laws.



📚 Background in Simple Words
💡 Before 2011, Customs and Excise officers treated offences as non-bailable & cognisable and arrested without warrants.
🧑‍⚖️ Om Prakash v. Union of India (2011) stopped this, holding such offences bailable & non-cognisable.
⚙️ To restore enforcement power, Parliament amended the Customs Act (2012, 2013, 2019) and framed similar arrest provisions under the GST Act (2017).
📩 Petitioners led by Radhika Agarwal claimed these arrests violated Arts. 14, 20(3), 21 & 22 and were used for coercive recoveries.

❓ Issues Before the Court
1️⃣ Whether arrest without FIR under Customs/GST violates Arts. 21 & 22.
2️⃣ Are Customs/GST officers “police officers”?
3️⃣ Do CrPC safeguards apply?
4️⃣ Is anticipatory bail possible without FIR?
5️⃣ Does Parliament have competence under Art. 246A for arrest and summons provisions?

⚖️ Court’s Reasoning — Explained Simply
1️⃣ Customs/GST Officers ≠ Police Officers 🚔
They work to protect revenue, not maintain law & order.
They cannot file a charge sheet under sec. 173 CrPC; they must proceed by complaint.
Therefore, they aren’t “police officers,” but constitutional safeguards still apply.
2️⃣ CrPC Safeguards Are Now Mandatory ✅
Even if not police, these officers must follow CrPC steps:
📄 Arrest memo (sec. 41B) 
⚖️ Advocate access (sec. 41D) 
📞 Inform family (sec. 50A) 🏥 Health check (sec. 55A) 
⏱️ Magistrate within 24 hrs (sec. 167).
🔴 Any breach = illegal arrest violating Arts. 21 & 22.
3️⃣ “Reasons to Believe” Means Evidence, Not Suspicion 📊
Under sec. 104 Customs Act, arrest needs documented evidence, not mere doubt.
Officers must record why they believe a serious offence (evasion > ₹50 lakh) occurred.
4️⃣ Kejriwal Safeguards 💬 Apply to GST/Customs
Borrowing from Arvind Kejriwal v. ED (2025) (PMLA case):
✍️ Written reasons must exist,
📑 Written grounds must be given to the arrestee,
📂 Arrest must be based on actual material.
🔸 Any arrest without these = unconstitutional.
5️⃣ Amendments Cured Om Prakash Gap ⚙️
Post-2012 law defines clear offence categories and monetary thresholds, so earlier precedent (Om Prakash 2011) no longer applies.
6️⃣ GST Arrests → CrPC Also Applies 📘
The GST Act is not a complete code – secs. 4–5 CrPC govern procedure.
Only major frauds (> ₹5 crore under sec. 132(1)(a)–(d)) are non-bailable.
The Commissioner must record “reasons to believe” and quantify loss before arrest.
7️⃣ No Forced Tax Recovery 💰🚫
CBIC Instructions (2022) forbid collecting money during raids.
Any payment made under pressure is void and refundable.
8️⃣ Summons ≠ Self-Incrimination 🗣️
A person summoned under sec. 70 is not yet an accused, so Art. 20(3) does not apply.
Once formally accused, they cannot be compelled to testify against themselves.
9️⃣ Anticipatory Bail Allowed 🕊️
Following Sushila Aggarwal (2020), anticipatory bail is permitted on reasonable fear of arrest — FIR is not required.
🔟 Parliament’s Power Under Art. 246A Upheld 🏛️
Under the Doctrine of Pith and Substance, arrest and summons powers are ancillary to GST collection; thus valid and constitutional.

💡 Key Doctrines & Principles Applied
⚖️ Doctrine of Pith and Substance – Focus on true purpose of law; enforcement powers support GST collection.
⚖️ Doctrine of Proportionality – Arrest must be a last resort and not punitive.
⚖️ Doctrine of Reasonable Procedure (Art. 21) – Every arrest must follow fair and non-arbitrary steps.
🗣️ Audi Alteram Partem – Right to know written grounds ensures defence opportunity.
📜 Substantive Due Process – Drawn from Maneka Gandhi (1978) to test fairness of procedure.

⚖️ Ratio Decidendi 🧠
Arrest under Customs and GST laws is constitutionally valid but only when carried out with strict compliance to CrPC safeguards and written, reasoned justification.
Arbitrary or coercive arrests violate Arts. 14, 21 and 22 of the Constitution.

🧾 Final Directions of the Court
1️⃣ Arrest powers under Customs & GST Acts are valid ✅.
2️⃣ Arrestee must get ✍️ written reasons and grounds of arrest.
3️⃣ Arrest = exception, not rule ⚖️.
4️⃣ Coercive recoveries 💰🚫 are illegal & refundable.
5️⃣ Anticipatory bail 🕊️ available on reasonable apprehension of arrest.
6️⃣ Magistrates must verify compliance before granting custody.

📚 Important Precedents & Why They Matter
1️⃣ State of Punjab v. Barkat Ram (1962) – Customs officers not “police officers.”
2️⃣ Ramesh Chandra Mehta (1969) – Inquiry ≠ accusation until complaint filed.
3️⃣ Tofan Singh (2020) – NDPS officers = police; draws contrast with revenue officers.
4️⃣ Om Prakash (2011) – Earlier made Customs offences bailable; now legislatively refined.
5️⃣ Deepak Mahajan (1994) – Non-police arrests need Magistrate within 24 hrs.
6️⃣ Arvind Kejriwal (2025) – Written reasons & grounds of arrest compulsory under PMLA; extended here.
7️⃣ Sushila Aggarwal (2020) – Anticipatory bail can be granted without FIR.
8️⃣ Poolpandi (1992) – Summons under tax laws valid; Art. 20(3) applies post accusation.
9️⃣ Atiq Begum (1941 FC) – Pith and substance doctrine upheld ancillary powers.
🔟 Maneka Gandhi (1978) – Every procedure under Art. 21 must be fair, just & reasonable.

🏁 Conclusion ✨
The Supreme Court upheld the arrest powers under Customs and GST laws ✅ but transformed their use through strong judicial safeguards ⚖️  requiring written reasons, CrPC compliance, and protection of personal liberty under Arts. 14, 21 & 22.
The ruling balances state authority and citizen freedom in India’s fiscal framework.

State of Tamil Nadu v. Governor of Tamil Nadu (2025 INSC 652)
📜 Theme: Limits of gubernatorial discretion and time-bound constitutional duty to act on Bills and Cabinet advice under Articles 153 – 164 & 200.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice J.B. Pardiwala & Justice R. Mahadevan
📅 Judgment Date: 8 April 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Writ Petitions (Civil) Nos. 1239 & 1271 of 2023 — filed by the State of Tamil Nadu under Article 32.

🏛️ Background of the Case
The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly passed multiple Bills (2020-2023) and sent them to the Governor for assent.
 The Governor neither approved nor returned several Bills for over a year, and later withheld assent to 10 of them.
 After the Assembly re-enacted those Bills, the Governor again reserved them for the President — creating a constitutional deadlock.
The State approached the Supreme Court, arguing that such inaction amounted to a “pocket veto”, violated Articles 200 and 163, and undermined the very fabric of parliamentary democracy.

📜 Relevant Provisions (Explained Simply)
Article 153 – Each State shall have a Governor as constitutional head.


Article 163(1) – Governor acts on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers except where the Constitution expressly gives discretion.


Article 164(2) – Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly.


Article 200 – When a Bill is presented, the Governor may ✔️ assent, ✖️ withhold assent, 🔁 return it once for reconsideration, or 📩 reserve it for the President — and must do so “as soon as possible.”


Article 142 – Empowers the Supreme Court to pass orders to do “complete justice.”



❓ Issues Before the Court
1️⃣ Can a Governor withhold or delay assent indefinitely (i.e., exercise a “pocket veto”)?
 2️⃣ Is the Governor bound by the Council of Ministers’ advice in matters of assent and administration?
 3️⃣ Can a Governor reserve for the President a Bill that has been re-enacted by the Assembly?
 4️⃣ What time-limits and accountability mechanisms exist under Article 200?
 5️⃣ Does judicial review extend to gubernatorial inaction and constitutional delays?

⚖️ Arguments
🔹 For the State of Tamil Nadu
Governor’s prolonged silence and re-reservation amounted to constitutional subversion.


Article 200 does not permit indefinite delay — it creates a duty to act.


Governor is bound by Cabinet advice (Art. 163); he is not a political veto point.


🔹 For the Governor / Union of India
Reservation was allegedly to examine repugnancy with Central laws.


Discretion exists to ensure constitutional compliance and preserve federal harmony.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ No Pocket Veto Permissible
 The phrase “as soon as possible” in Article 200 is mandatory. Indefinite delay or silence is a dereliction of constitutional duty.
 The Governor is not an alternative executive centre but a symbolic head bound to respect the legislature’s will.
2️⃣ Duty to Act on Aid and Advice
 Following Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab (1974), the Governor must ordinarily act on ministerial advice.
 Discretion is narrow — for example, under Articles 356 or 371; not for routine Bills or appointments.
3️⃣ Reservation After Re-enactment is Illegal
 Once a Bill returned by the Governor is re-passed by the Assembly, the Governor is constitutionally bound to assent.
 Re-reservation after re-enactment nullifies legislative sovereignty and was declared ultra vires Article 200.
4️⃣ Timelines for Constitutional Action ⏰
 To end governance stalemates, the Court laid down specific deadlines:
Decision on Cabinet advice → within 1 month.


If withholding assent / returning Bill → within 3 months with written message.


If Assembly re-enacts Bill → assent mandatory within 1 month.


5️⃣ Governor’s Duty to Uphold Representative Democracy
 Delaying assent erodes collective responsibility (Art. 164) and amounts to a constitutional breakdown.
6️⃣ Use of Article 142 — Deemed Assent
 Invoking its power to do complete justice, the Court deemed the 10 Bills to have received assent since the Governor’s inaction was unconstitutional.

📚 Key Precedents Relied Upon
Shamsher Singh v. State of Punjab (1974) – Governor acts on aid and advice.


Nabam Rebia v. Deputy Speaker (2016) – Discretion does not extend to legislative processes.


Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India (2006) – Governor’s satisfaction is reviewable.


B.P. Singhal v. Union of India (2010) – Governor’s office is constitutional, not political.


Kehar Singh v. Union of India (1989) – Executive discretion must reflect constitutional morality.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
1️⃣ Governor must act on aid and advice of Council of Ministers except where explicit discretion exists.
 2️⃣ Indefinite withholding or re-reservation of Bills is unconstitutional.
 3️⃣ Article 200 imposes a time-bound duty, not a power to stall.
 4️⃣ Failure to act within reasonable time is subject to judicial review.
 5️⃣ Where Governor fails to decide after re-enactment, assent is deemed granted.

🧾 Judgment & Directions
✅ Declared Governor’s inaction illegal and erroneous.
 ✅ Held Bills deemed assented under Article 142.
 ✅ Directed Governors to act within prescribed timelines.
 ✅ Mandated Union and State Governments to frame uniform guidelines ensuring constitutional compliance.
 ✅ Reaffirmed that inaction = constitutional violation open to judicial review.

💡 Doctrinal Insights & Constitutional Principles
Doctrine of Constitutional Morality 🧭 – All functionaries must exercise powers with fidelity to constitutional values, not political interests.


Doctrine of Constitutional Trust 🤝 – Governor and Council stand in a relationship of mutual trust; Governor is a facilitator of governance, not an obstructionist.


Rule of Law ⚖️ – Every constitutional office is accountable to law; delay without reason is arbitrary.


Federal Balance 🏛️ – Judgment protects State autonomy from central interference via Governors.


Judicial Review as Basic Structure 🔍 – Courts retain power to correct constitutional inertia.



🧠 Practical Implications
Creates binding timelines for Governor’s actions under Article 200.


Ends the practice of “pocket vetoes.”


Strengthens federalism and collective responsibility.


Introduces accountability mechanisms for constitutional heads.


Provides clear guidance for future litigation on executive inaction.



📚 Key Takeaway for CLAT PG & Judiciary Aspirants
This case is the Indian equivalent of the “Governor’s assent controversy” precedent.
 Remember these keywords:
 No pocket veto 🛑 | Time-bound assent ⏱️ | Aid & Advice binding 📜 | Constitutional Morality 🧭 | Federal Balance 🏛️ | Judicial Review 🔍

🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
State of Tamil Nadu v. Governor of Tamil Nadu (2025) cemented that the Governor is a constitutional head bound by Cabinet advice and must act within a reasonable time under Article 200.
 Indefinite inaction is a constitutional breach, and Bills re-enacted by the legislature receive deemed assent under Article 142, ensuring that democratic will remains supreme over executive delay. ⚖️🏛️

Sunil Kumar Singh v. Bihar Legislative Assembly (2025 INSC 578)
📜 Theme:
 Judicial review of legislative expulsions — scope of parliamentary privilege under Articles 194 and 212, and the constitutional limits of internal autonomy of State legislatures.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice B.R. Gavai
 Justice C.T. Ravikumar
📅 Judgment Date: 22 April 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Civil Writ Petition under Article 32 challenging expulsion resolution passed by the Bihar Legislative Assembly.

📘 Background
Sunil Kumar Singh, an MLA from Bihar, was expelled by the Bihar Legislative Assembly for allegedly “breaching the dignity of the House” after he made public allegations of corruption against the Speaker.
 The Assembly’s Ethics Committee found him guilty of “gross misconduct” and recommended expulsion, which the House adopted by majority vote.
Singh challenged the decision before the Supreme Court, arguing that the expulsion was politically motivated and violated his fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19(1)(a), and 21.
 He also argued that legislative privilege cannot override constitutional supremacy or judicial review.

📜 Relevant Constitutional Provisions (Explained Simply)
Article 194(3): Grants State legislatures the same powers, privileges, and immunities as the House of Commons (until defined by law).


Article 212(1): Courts shall not question the validity of any legislative proceeding on grounds of procedural irregularity.


Article 226/32: Courts’ power of judicial review — safeguard against constitutional excesses by any organ.


Article 19(1)(a): Freedom of speech, which extends to public criticism of government conduct.


Article 14: Equality and fairness in State action apply even to legislatures exercising quasi-judicial powers.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Whether Article 212 bars judicial review of a legislative expulsion resolution.


Whether the Bihar Legislative Assembly exceeded its privilege powers under Article 194(3).


Whether the expulsion violated fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19(1)(a), and 21.


What is the scope of judicial review over internal proceedings of State legislatures?



⚖️ Arguments
🔹 Petitioner (Sunil Kumar Singh):
Article 212 bars review of procedural irregularities, not substantive illegality or constitutional violations.


Expulsion for exposing corruption is an attack on free speech and democracy.


The action was disproportionate and politically motivated — violating Articles 14 and 19(1)(a).


🔹 Respondent (Bihar Legislative Assembly):
Article 194(3) grants the Assembly exclusive power to regulate its internal proceedings.


Judicial review would violate separation of powers.


The House is the sole judge of its privileges and dignity.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ Nature of Legislative Privilege
The Court reaffirmed that privileges are constitutional powers meant to ensure independent legislative functioning — not immunity from constitutional scrutiny.
 They exist to protect institutional integrity, not to punish dissent or suppress criticism.
2️⃣ Scope of Article 212
Article 212 bars courts from examining “procedural irregularities” within legislative proceedings, but not substantive illegality or constitutional violations.
 Hence, if the legislature acts mala fide, ultra vires, or in violation of fundamental rights, courts can intervene.
 The bar under Article 212 is procedural, not jurisdictional.
3️⃣ Application of Raja Ram Pal v. Lok Sabha (2007)
The Bench relied on Raja Ram Pal, which held that judicial review extends to examining the legality, not merits, of parliamentary privileges.
 Courts may assess:
(a) whether the action has a jurisdictional basis under Article 194(3);


(b) whether constitutional limitations (Articles 14, 19, 21) are breached;


(c) whether there is procedural fairness.


4️⃣ Distinction Between “Privilege” and “Power”
The Court distinguished between:
Privileges: Powers to protect legislative independence (e.g., freedom of speech in the House).


Punitive Powers: Sanctioning members, which must be exercised within constitutional bounds.


The Assembly’s power to punish cannot be used to silence opposition voices or criticism.
5️⃣ Proportionality and Reasonableness
Expulsion is the most severe legislative penalty.
 Applying the Doctrine of Proportionality, the Court found the punishment excessive, as the MLA’s conduct did not obstruct legislative business or cause disorder.
6️⃣ Free Speech and Democratic Accountability
The Court highlighted that legislators retain Article 19(1)(a) rights outside the House.
 Publicly exposing corruption is protected political speech; punishment for such speech undermines transparency and democratic debate.

📚 Key Precedents Cited
Raja Ram Pal v. Speaker, Lok Sabha (2007) 3 SCC 184 – Judicial review permissible for substantive illegality in legislative privilege cases.


Amarinder Singh v. Punjab Vidhan Sabha (2010) 6 SCC 113 – Expulsion cannot be used as a political weapon; must relate to breach of privilege.


Kihoto Hollohan v. Zachillhu (1992) Supp (2) SCC 651 – Speaker’s decisions under Tenth Schedule subject to judicial review on mala fides or illegality.


Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975) Supp SCC 1 – Judicial review is part of basic structure.


Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association (2016) 5 SCC 1 – Separation of powers does not exclude judicial oversight.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
Judicial review under Articles 32 and 226 is not barred by Article 212 — courts may intervene in cases of substantive illegality, mala fides, or constitutional violations.


The privilege of expulsion under Article 194(3) must be exercised only for genuine breach of privilege, not as a disciplinary or political tool.


Legislative privileges are subject to the Constitution — they cannot override Articles 14, 19, and 21.


The Doctrine of Proportionality applies to legislative punishments, ensuring fairness and democratic accountability.



🧾 Judgment / Directions
The expulsion of Sunil Kumar Singh was declared unconstitutional and void.


The MLA was reinstated with all consequential benefits.


The Court directed State Legislatures to frame clear rules defining:


“breach of privilege,”


“contempt of House,” and


proportional penalties.


The Supreme Court recommended the formation of a National Model Framework on Legislative Privileges, under the Ministry of Law and Justice, to standardize norms across States.



💡 Doctrinal Insights
Doctrine
Application
Basic Structure Doctrine
Judicial review cannot be excluded by Article 212.
Doctrine of Proportionality
Legislative punishment must be fair and necessary.
Separation of Powers
Legislatures have autonomy, but not absolute immunity.
Substantive vs Procedural Illegality
Only procedural irregularities barred from review.
Constitutional Supremacy
All privileges subject to fundamental rights.


🧩 Conceptual Significance for CLAT PG
Defines limits of Article 212 immunity → procedural only, not substantive.


Clarifies relationship between Article 194(3) privileges and fundamental rights.


Reinforces judicial review as part of basic structure.


Introduces proportionality test in legislative discipline.



🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
Sunil Kumar Singh v. Bihar Legislative Assembly (2025) reaffirmed that judicial review extends even to legislative expulsions — Article 212 shields only procedural irregularities, not constitutional violations. Legislative privileges must operate within the bounds of Articles 14, 19, and 21, preserving democracy’s accountability. ⚖️🏛️

Union of India v. National Human Rights Commission (2025 INSC 345)
📜 Theme:
 Scope and enforceability of NHRC’s recommendatory powers — balancing executive accountability, constitutional duties under Article 21, and the institutional autonomy of human rights commissions.

👩‍⚖️ Bench
Justice Sanjay Karol
 Justice Manoj Misra
📅 Judgment Date: 14 March 2025
 ⚖️ Jurisdiction: Civil Appeal under Article 136 of the Constitution

📘 Background of the Case
The case arose after the NHRC directed the Union Ministry of Home Affairs to pay compensation to victims of custodial torture in Assam and to prosecute errant officials.
The Union government partially complied but refused full compensation, arguing that NHRC’s orders were “recommendatory” and thus not binding.
NHRC challenged this interpretation before the Supreme Court, asserting that persistent executive non-compliance undermined the constitutional guarantee of life and dignity under Article 21.
The case, therefore, became a constitutional test of how far a “statutory watchdog” can go in compelling the State to act.

📜 Relevant Provisions and Doctrines (Explained Simply)
Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
Section 12: Lists NHRC’s functions, including inquiry into violations, recommending prosecutions, and suggesting reforms.


Section 18: Allows NHRC to recommend compensation, disciplinary action, or prosecution in cases of rights violations.


Section 19: Requires the Government to “inform NHRC of action taken” within one month — this formed the core interpretive question.


Constitutional Provisions
Article 21: Right to life includes protection from torture and inhuman treatment.


Article 51A(e): Fundamental duty to uphold human dignity.


Articles 73 & 162: Define executive power of Union and States.


Article 144: All authorities must act in aid of the Supreme Court — reinforcing constitutional hierarchy.



❓ Issues Before the Court
Are NHRC’s recommendations binding on the Government?


Can the Government reject NHRC recommendations without recorded reasons?


Does NHRC have power to direct compensation or prosecution?


What is the scope of judicial review if governments ignore NHRC’s findings?



⚖️ Arguments
For the Union of India:
NHRC’s powers are recommendatory, not adjudicatory.


Section 18 uses “recommend” — implying advisory intent.


Binding effect would amount to judicial overreach into executive functioning.


The executive alone decides compensation and disciplinary action.


For NHRC:
The purpose of the Act is to protect human dignity under Article 21 — non-compliance makes it meaningless.


Section 19 requires a written response within one month, showing legislative intent for mandatory consideration, not optional review.


NHRC cannot be reduced to a paper tiger; otherwise, constitutional accountability collapses.



⚖️ Court’s Analysis and Reasoning
1️⃣ Constitutional Status of NHRC
 The Court declared NHRC a “statutory-constitutional hybrid body” — though created by statute, it enforces constitutional mandates under Articles 14, 21, and 51A.
 It derives its moral authority from the constitutional conscience of human dignity, not merely from legislative text.
2️⃣ Binding Nature of Recommendations
 The Court held that while NHRC cannot execute its orders like a civil court, its recommendations are “binding in spirit” — meaning:
 The Government must either (a) comply, or (b) record cogent written reasons for non-compliance within one month under Section 19.
 Silence or mechanical rejection is unconstitutional.
3️⃣ Duty to Act under Section 19
 “Inform the Commission of action taken” implies substantive, reasoned response.
 Failure to do so violates the principles of natural justice and the victim’s right to information.
4️⃣ Constitutional Obligation to Protect Life and Dignity
 Citing Nilabati Behera v. State of Orissa (1993) and D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997), the Court held that compensation for rights violations is not charity — it’s a constitutional remedy.
 NHRC’s recommendations thus embody Article 21 obligations.
5️⃣ Judicial Review of Executive Inaction
 Judicial review remains open if non-compliance with NHRC directions is arbitrary, mala fide, or unreasonable.
 High Courts under Article 226 can compel compliance or seek written justifications.
6️⃣ Transformative Interpretation
 The Court applied the doctrine of transformative constitutionalism — reading the 1993 Act as a dynamic tool for enforcing human dignity and accountability, consistent with India’s international obligations under the UN Paris Principles (1993).

📚 Important Precedents Cited
Nilabati Behera v. State of Orissa (1993) – Compensation for custodial death is a constitutional obligation.


D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997) – Preventive guidelines against torture; NHRC’s oversight affirmed.


N.C. Dhoundial v. Union of India (2004) – Earlier view treating NHRC’s role as advisory distinguished here.


People’s Union for Civil Liberties v. Union of India (2005) – NHRC’s monitoring powers in human rights cases upheld.


K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) – Reinforced dignity and privacy as facets of Article 21.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
NHRC’s recommendations under Section 18 of the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993, though not judicially enforceable as decrees, have binding persuasive force.
 The executive is constitutionally obligated to comply or justify non-compliance with written reasons.
 Failure to act amounts to arbitrary inaction, violating Articles 14 and 21.
NHRC thus occupies a constitutional oversight role, ensuring that the State’s coercive powers remain accountable to the rule of law.

🧾 Judgment and Directions
Governments must respond to NHRC recommendations within one month, with reasons for non-implementation.


NHRC shall create an online compliance tracker showing action taken by Union and State governments.


Non-compliance without reasons shall be treated as prima facie contempt of constitutional duties.


High Courts may issue mandamus or directions in cases of prolonged inaction.


The Ministry of Home Affairs was directed to designate nodal human rights officers for compliance coordination.



💡 Doctrinal Insights and CLAT-PG Application
This case strengthens four constitutional doctrines:
Constitutional Accountability: Even non-judicial bodies can impose binding constitutional obligations when enforcing Article 21 rights.


Rule of Law: Executive discretion is subject to written justification — non-action is arbitrary.


Binding Persuasion Doctrine: NHRC’s findings create a mandatory duty to act or explain.


Transformative Constitutionalism: Statutes like the 1993 Act must evolve to fulfil fundamental rights and India’s international human rights commitments.



🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
Union of India v. National Human Rights Commission (2025) transformed NHRC from an advisory watchdog into a constitutionally empowered accountability institution.
 Its recommendations now carry binding persuasive authority, and governments must comply or explain, making human rights enforcement an operational facet of Article 21 and the rule of law. ⚖️🕊️

Union of India v. Rajasthan High Court (2025 INSC 102)
📜 Theme: Reaffirmed judicial independence and clarified administrative control of High Courts under Articles 229, 235, and 309 of the Constitution.

👩‍⚖️ Bench:
Chief Justice Sanjiv Khanna,
 Justice Manoj Misra,
 Justice P.V. Sanjay Kumar
📅 Judgment Date: January 29, 2025
⚖️Jurisdiction: Civil Appeal under Article 136 (Special Leave Petition).

📘 Background of the Case
This case arose from a conflict between the Rajasthan High Court and the State Government regarding the appointment and service conditions of High Court staff.
The Rajasthan Government attempted to modify recruitment and promotion rules of High Court employees using its executive power under Article 309, while the High Court asserted its exclusive control under Article 229 of the Constitution.
The Union of India intervened after a division bench judgment struck down certain State amendments as violating the independence of the judiciary.

📜 Relevant Constitutional Provisions (Explained Simply)
Art. 229 🏛️ – Chief Justice of a High Court has exclusive power over appointment and conditions of service of court officers and servants.


Art. 235 ⚖️ – Control over district and subordinate judiciary vested in the High Court.


Art. 309 📜 – Empowers Parliament or State legislatures to regulate service conditions of public servants.


Art. 50 ⚖️ – Directive Principle mandating separation of judiciary from executive.


Art. 14 & 21 – Ensure equality and fairness in administrative decision-making.



❓ Issues Before the Court
1️⃣ Whether the State Government can unilaterally amend service rules of High Court staff without consulting the Chief Justice.
 2️⃣ Whether the Chief Justice’s administrative authority under Art. 229 is absolute or subject to State concurrence.
 3️⃣ To what extent does Article 309 permit executive rule-making affecting the judiciary.
 4️⃣ Whether judicial independence includes administrative independence over High Court staff.

⚖️ Court’s Analysis & Reasoning
1️⃣ Article 229 is a Constitutional Safeguard for Judicial Independence
 The Court held that Art. 229 vests the entire administrative control of High Court staff in the Chief Justice.
 The only exception is financial — requiring prior approval of the Governor when expenditure involves the Consolidated Fund.
2️⃣ State Cannot Override Chief Justice’s Authority
 The Rajasthan Government’s amendments under Art. 309 were struck down.
 The Court noted that executive rule-making cannot encroach upon the High Court’s domain of internal administration.
3️⃣ Administrative Independence = Judicial Independence
 The Court reiterated that independence of the judiciary includes institutional and administrative autonomy.
 Interference in staff control, transfers, or promotions indirectly compromises judicial independence — a basic feature of the Constitution.
4️⃣ Role of the Governor Is Formal, Not Discretionary
 When the Governor acts under Art. 229, he does so on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers and in consultation with the Chief Justice.
 It is a constitutional formality, not an independent power.
5️⃣ Separation of Powers Doctrine Reinforced
 The executive cannot “regulate” the judiciary’s internal affairs, as such power disturbs the balance of powers under Articles 50 and 229.
 Judicial independence means freedom not only from executive pressure in decisions but also from administrative subordination.

💡 Doctrines & Principles Applied
Doctrine of Separation of Powers – Distinguishes functional domains of executive and judiciary.


Doctrine of Basic Structure – Judicial independence forms part of the Constitution’s basic structure.


Doctrine of Institutional Autonomy – High Courts must have control over their personnel to maintain credibility and impartiality.


Doctrine of Constitutional Supremacy – Executive rules cannot override constitutional provisions.



⚖️ Ratio Decidendi
The Chief Justice of a High Court has exclusive constitutional authority under Art. 229 to regulate appointment, promotion, and service conditions of High Court staff.
 Executive interference, even through Art. 309 rules, violates judicial independence — a basic feature of the Constitution.

🧾 Judgment / Directions
1️⃣ Rajasthan Government’s amendments under Art. 309 declared unconstitutional.
 2️⃣ Reaffirmed that High Courts are administratively independent of the State executive.
 3️⃣ State Governments must obtain Chief Justice’s concurrence before issuing any rule impacting High Court staff.
 4️⃣ Administrative autonomy of judiciary is an essential element of judicial independence.
 5️⃣ Union and States directed to harmonize their service rules with Art. 229.

📚 Key Precedents Cited
1️⃣ High Court of Judicature for Rajasthan v. Ramesh Chand Paliwal (1998) – Reiterated Art. 229 gives complete control to Chief Justice over High Court staff.
 2️⃣ Supreme Court Employees’ Welfare Association v. Union of India (1989) – Judicial independence includes administrative autonomy.
 3️⃣ All India Judges Association v. Union of India (1993) – Judicial service conditions integral to independence.
 4️⃣ State of Bihar v. Bal Mukund Sah (2000) – Governor’s approval under Art. 229 is formal, not discretionary.
 5️⃣ Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India (2015) – Institutional independence is part of the basic structure.

🏁 Conclusion (One-Line Takeaway)
The Supreme Court reaffirmed that administrative independence is inseparable from judicial independence, the Chief Justice, not the executive, controls High Court staff.
 Any State rule under Art. 309 inconsistent with Art. 229 is ultra vires the Constitution. ⚖️🏛️
